content.problemStatement:
Chef is setting up a new restaurant in Chefland, and has to decide on a name for it.

In Chefland, all restaurants must have names that are of length **exactly** NNN, consisting of lowercase English letters.

Unfortunately for Chef, there's a competing restaurant right across the street!

This competitor has a name represented by the string AAA, which consists of only lowercase English letters.

Chef believes that bigger is better — so he wants his restaurant's name to be lexicographically larger†^\dagger† than AAA.

In fact, to go even further beyond, he wants to be larger no matter which direction the name is read in: so he also wants the reverse of his restaurant's name to be lexicographically larger than AAA.

Help Chef find any valid name for his restaurant, or tell him that no valid names exist.

†^\dagger† String PPP of length NNN is lexicographically larger than another string QQQ of the same length if and only if there exists an index iii (1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N) such that:


Pi>QiP_i \gt Q_iPi​>Qi​; and
Pj=QjP_j = Q_jPj​=Qj​ for all 1≤j<i1 \leq j \lt i1≤j<i

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of restaurant names in Chefland.
The second line contains a string AAA of length NNN, the name of the competing restaurant.




Output Format
For each test case,



If no solution exists, print the single integer −1-1−1 on a new line.
Otherwise, output a single string of length NNN, consisting of lowercase English letters: a valid name for Chef's restaurant.

If there are multiple possible answers, any of them will be accepted.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: "topcoder" is lexicographically larger than "codechef". The reverse of "topcoder" is "redocpot" which is also lexicographically larger than "codechef".
So, this is a valid name for the restaurant.
Test case 333: It can be proved that no valid name exists.
